title: Exodus 29
subtitle: The Ordination Ceremony; the Daily Offering; I Will Dwell Among You

sections.header: Verses 1–14 — The Ordination Ceremony; the Sin Offering

notes.note: MacArthur: The ordination procedure takes seven days -- a complete cycle, a new creation. Washing (cleansing), clothing (covering), anointing (divine enabling), sacrifice (atonement maintained).

MacArthur: Take the anointing oil and anoint him by pouring it on his head. The anointing of the priest is the origin of the title Messiah. The high priest anointed for his mediating role prefigures the ultimate High Priest anointed with the Spirit without measure (John 3:34).

heb.paragraph: V.29: The ordination ceremony for priests is called millûʾîm — literally "fillings." To ordain is to fill: the priest is "filled" for the role, equipped and commissioned. The image of divine filling (mālēʾ) runs through Scripture: the Spirit fills the craftsmen (35:31), Joshua (Deut 34:9), and at Pentecost the disciples (Acts 2:4) — all ordinations, all enablments for a role in God's service.

V.7,21: The anointing of Aaron with the sacred oil (shemen hammishḥāh) is the central act of priestly ordination. Māshaḥ (to smear, anoint) gives us "Messiah" (māshîaḥ) and "Christ" (christos — "anointed one"). The king (1 Sam 16:13), the High Priest, and occasionally the prophet were all anointed. Jesus received the Spirit without measure (John 3:34) — the ultimate anointing that fulfilled and surpassed all priestly, prophetic, and royal anointings.

hist.historical: The ordination ceremony follows a consecration pattern attested in Mesopotamian and Hittite priestly installation rituals: washing, vesting, anointing, and sacrifice. The anointing with oil (mĕšîḥâ) is the source of the term 'messiah' (māšîaḥ, 'anointed one'). The sin offering (ḥaṭṭāʾt) — a bull whose blood is applied to the altar horns — addresses ritual impurity. Placing hands on the animal's head (sĕmîkâ) transfers identification; the Hittite rituals of Ašḫella describe a similar transfer-of-impurity procedure.
hist.context: The seven-day ordination ceremony mirrors creation's seven days — the priesthood is a new creation, a new beginning of ordered relationship with God. The three sacrifices (sin offering/bull, burnt offering/ram, ordination offering/ram) move from atonement to dedication to celebration: the priests are first cleansed of guilt, then wholly given to God, then established in the fellowship of the sacred meal.

refs.note: The anointing oil running down Aaron's beard and onto his collar — the Psalm uses this as the image of brotherly unity.
Christ's once-for-all sacrifice contrasted with the priests' daily/annual sacrifices — the ordination of Exod 29 points to the final high priest.
The execution of the ordination ceremony as commanded here — Exod 29 gives the instructions; Lev 8 records the performance.

sarna.source: Nahum Sarna, JPS Torah Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The washing (rachatz) that begins the ordination is a purification ritual that removes ritual contamination. Sarna notes that priestly purification rites are attested throughout the ancient Near East — the unique feature of Israel's version is its connection to covenant appointment rather than merely cultic cleansing. Aaron and his sons are washed into a new status, not merely cleaned from an old one.

The anointing oil (shemen hamishchah) poured over Aaron's head is the act that makes him 'the anointed priest' (hakohen hamashiach, Lev 4:3). Sarna notes that anointing was used in the ancient Near East for the consecration of kings, priests, and sacred objects. The oil poured on Aaron's head makes him a mashiach (anointed one) — the same word applied to kings and prophets, and ultimately to the Messiah.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The ordination sequence is a physical transformation narrative: the ordinary man is washed, clothed in sacred garments, and anointed with oil. Alter notes the sequence moves from the body (washing, dressing) to the person's status (anointing). The ceremony is not merely preparation for service; it is a constitutive act that creates the priest. Without the ceremony, there is no priest; after it, there is.

The sin offering's disposal — blood on the horns, fat on the altar, flesh outside the camp — is the most complex of the ordination sacrifices. Alter notes the spatial movement: the most sacred elements (blood, fat) go to the altar; the remainder goes outside the camp. The spatial contrast between inside (holy) and outside (expelled) will develop throughout Leviticus. Christ's death 'outside the gate' (Heb 13:12) reverses the spatial logic: the holy One goes outside to bear the expelled.

Calvin on the ordination ceremony: 'These were not empty ceremonies without any spiritual meaning; for God had one end in view — to direct his people to Christ.' The three acts — washing, clothing, anointing — are for Calvin the OT form of the Christian's reception of the Holy Spirit: 'The priests were washed that they might represent that purity and holiness of life which is required; clothed, that they might sustain the honour of the office; anointed, that they might be endued with spiritual gifts for its discharge.'

Calvin on blood at the altar horns: 'As the blood was the symbol of expiation, this was a confirmation that the altar was purified by blood; and that God was only to be approached through an expiatory sacrifice.' The ordination ceremony is itself a sacrificial act — the priests are consecrated through the same blood-altar structure that governs all subsequent priestly service. The system's beginning is the system's pattern.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note on anointing the priest discusses the anointing oil formula (30:22–25): myrrh, cinnamon, cane, and cassia in olive oil. The note explains that the anointing of the high priest is described as oil 'poured' on the head (Ps 133:2), while the anointing of ordinary priests may have been a sprinkling on the garments (Lev 8:30). The high priest's anointing is the most abundant and the most visible — the oil flowing over the garments as the ceremony proceeds.

The NET note on the sin offering bull discusses the laying of hands on the bull's head before slaughter — the identification gesture. The note traces the theological mechanics: the priest identifies himself with the sacrifice; the sacrifice bears what the priest brings to it. This is the foundational gesture of substitutionary atonement.

sections.header: Verses 15–37 — Burnt Offering and Ordination Ram; Seven-Day Consecration

notes.note: MacArthur: The blood applied to Aaron's right earlobe, right thumb, and right big toe consecrates the priest's hearing, doing, and going. The complete body consecration signifies total dedication to covenant service.

MacArthur: The atonement offerings of the ordination are for Aaron and his sons -- the priests must be personally atoned for before they can atone for others. No priest serves from personal holiness sufficient for the task.

heb.paragraph: V.14,36: The ḥaṭṭāʾt (sin/purification offering) was specifically designed to deal with inadvertent or ritual uncleanness. The word comes from the root ḥāṭāʾ (to miss the mark, to sin). The offering purifies the sanctuary as well as the offerer — a concept crucial to understanding why Christ's death was necessary not just for individual sinners but for the cosmic restoration of the defiled creation (cf. Heb 9:23; Rom 8:20-21).

V.28: The shĕlāmîm (peace/fellowship offering) was unique in that the worshipper ate part of the sacrifice. It was a shared meal between God, the priests, and the offerer. The root shālôm (peace, wholeness, completeness) names both the offering and its goal: restored relationship, shared table, communal harmony. The Lord's Supper is the new covenant shĕlāmîm — the shared meal that enacts and celebrates peace between God and his people.

hist.historical: The ram of ordination (ʾêl hammilluʾîm, 'ram of filling') gets its name from the act of 'filling the hands' — placing portions of the sacrifice in the priests' hands. This gesture is attested in Akkadian texts as a formal act of investiture (mullû qātam). The application of blood to the right ear, thumb, and big toe consecrates the priest's hearing (to hear God's word), action (to perform God's service), and walk (to follow God's path). The seven-day consecration period matches the duration of temple-dedication festivals at Gudea's temple in Lagash (c. 2100 BC).
hist.context: The blood on right ear, thumb, and big toe (v.20) is the ordination's most distinctive element. It does not appear in any other sacrificial context — it is unique to priestly consecration. The same rite is used in the cleansing of lepers (Lev 14:14), signifying complete restoration. The priest is entirely consecrated: everything he hears, everything he does, everywhere he goes is claimed by God.

refs.note: Leviticus 8 records the actual performance of this ordination ritual. The seven-day consecration period establishes a pattern: holiness is not instantaneous but requires sustained ritual preparation.

Hebrews contrasts the repeated consecration sacrifices with Christ's single offering: 'Unlike the other high priests, he does not need to offer sacrifices day after day … he sacrificed for their sins once for all.'
Paul's 'offer your bodies as a living sacrifice' reinterprets the ordination-sacrifice language. What was literal animal sacrifice in Exodus becomes metaphorical self-offering in the New Testament.

sarna.source: Nahum Sarna, JPS Torah Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The daily (tamid) offering — two lambs, morning and evening — is the covenant's foundational sacrifice. Sarna notes the tamid ('perpetual/regular') offering structures Israelite time: morning and evening sacrifices bracket the day. The God of Israel is approached twice daily, every day, through the same prescribed sacrifice. The regularity is the theological statement: covenant worship is not occasional but perpetual, not emergency-driven but calendar-structured.

The concluding statement of the ordination instructions — 'I will dwell among the Israelites' — is the teleological statement of the entire tabernacle project. Sarna identifies this as the climax of the Exodus narrative: the purpose of liberation was not freedom per se but divine-human co-habitation. The tabernacle section that began with 'let them make me a sanctuary so that I may dwell among them' (25:8) finds its resolution here: the dwelling is coming; it will be accomplished.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The chapter's final verses are its theological summit. Alter notes that the long, technical chapters of tabernacle design and priestly ordination are framed by the simplest possible divine commitment: 'I will dwell among the Israelites and be their God.' All the complexity — the cubits, the materials, the priestly garments, the ordination sacrifices — exists to enable the most intimate possible relationship: God with his people, in their midst, as their God.

The 'meeting with the Israelites' promised at the tent's entrance — 'there I will meet with the Israelites, and the tabernacle will be sanctified by my glory' — is the architectural climax. Alter notes the tabernacle's purpose is not the building itself but the meetings it enables. God is building a place to meet with his people, not a monument to himself.

Calvin on divine dwelling: 'This is the chief happiness of men — that God should be their God, and that they should be his people.' The divine-human cohabitation is not a theological abstraction but the substance of covenant happiness. Calvin applies this to the Christian's experience of the Spirit's indwelling: what the tabernacle enacted locally and temporally, the Spirit enacts universally and permanently in the new covenant community.

Calvin: God dwells among his people not because he needs a house but because we need to know where to find him. The tabernacle is for Israel's benefit, not God's. It gives the covenant people a specific, accessible, reliable place to encounter the God who is everywhere — condescension to human limitation.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note on 'so that I might dwell among them' (leshokni betokham) connects to the tabernacle's name (mishkan, dwelling place). The note identifies this as the programmatic statement of the tabernacle section (25:8) repeated as its conclusion. The entire architectural and liturgical system described in chs.25–31 exists to achieve this single goal: divine indwelling among the human community. The NT's application (John 1:14: 'tabernacled among us'; Rev 21:3: 'God's dwelling is with humanity') extends this goal to its ultimate eschatological fulfilment.

The NET note on 'I will dwell among the Israelites and be their God' discusses this as the covenant formula in its most concentrated form. The note traces the formula through Lev 26:12, Ezek 37:27, and Rev 21:3 — from tabernacle to temple to eschatological fulfilment. The entire canon is the progressive realisation of this single promise.

sections.header: Verses 38–46 — The Daily Offering; "I Will Dwell Among the Israelites"

notes.note: MacArthur: I will dwell among the Israelites and be their God. The stated goal of the entire tabernacle project: divine dwelling in the midst of the covenant community. This is the covenant's ultimate promise, fulfilled progressively through tabernacle, temple, incarnation, Pentecost, and the new Jerusalem (Rev 21:3).

MacArthur: The daily burnt offerings — morning and evening — establish the rhythm that all subsequent covenant worship will follow. The tamid (regular offering) is not occasional but structural: twice daily, without interruption. God's dwelling with Israel is not crisis-response but sustained covenantal presence maintained by uninterrupted sacrifice.

heb.paragraph: V.38-42: "Offer two lambs a day as the regular burnt offering (ʿōlat tāmîd) — one in the morning, one at twilight." The daily sacrificial rhythm structured Israel's entire temporal existence around the offering of life to God. The priest who served at the morning and evening offering gave each day a sacrificial frame. Heb 7:27 draws the contrast: Christ "does not need to offer sacrifices day after day... he sacrificed for their sins once for all when he offered himself."

V.46: "They will know (yādĕʿû) that I am the LORD their God, who brought them out of Egypt so that I might dwell among them." The purpose of the Exodus and the Tabernacle is yĕdîʿāh — knowledge of God. But this is not cognitive knowledge: yādaʿ in Hebrew is covenantal, intimate, experiential knowledge (cf. Gen 4:1 where Adam "knew" Eve). The whole elaborate system of Tabernacle worship is designed to facilitate deep personal encounter with the living God.

hist.historical: The daily burnt offering — two lambs, morning and evening — established the tāmîd ('continual') sacrifice that became the heartbeat of Israelite worship for over a thousand years, continuing until the temple's destruction in AD 70. Daily offerings to the deity were standard in Mesopotamian and Egyptian temples; the temple of Amun at Karnak consumed vast quantities of bread, beer, meat, and incense daily. The closing promise — 'I will dwell among the Israelites and be their God' (v. 45) — is the theological climax: the tabernacle exists to facilitate divine presence.
hist.context: The daily burnt offering (vv.38–42) frames the Tabernacle's ongoing life: a lamb at dawn, a lamb at dusk. Every day began and ended with sacrifice. The people woke to the smoke of a burnt offering and went to sleep to the same. Their daily rhythm was punctuated by the reminder that approach to God requires the giving of life. Hebrews argues that Christ's single sacrifice replaced this perpetual offering — once for all instead of once every morning.

refs.note: Daily priests stand and offer repeatedly; Christ sat down after one offering. The daily sacrifice of Exod 29 is fulfilled and superseded.
"God's dwelling place is now among the people" — the final fulfilment of v.45.
"The Word became flesh and dwelt among us" — the first fulfilment of v.45.

sarna.source: Nahum Sarna, JPS Torah Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The sanctuary is 'sanctified by my glory' (veniqdash bikhvodi). Sarna notes the divine passive: the tabernacle's holiness is not inherent in its materials or construction but derived from the divine presence that inhabits it. The same principle appears in 40:34–35: the glory cloud fills the completed tabernacle, making it holy. Holiness always flows from the divine presence to the structure, never the reverse.

The triple consecration — of the tent, the altar, Aaron, and his sons — by divine glory rather than human action establishes that the tabernacle's sanctity is God's gift, not a human achievement. Sarna notes the covenant logic: God is the one who makes holy; the human beings are those who receive the holiness extended to them.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The chapter's conclusion zooms out from the specific (ordination sacrifices) to the general (the tabernacle as meeting place) to the cosmic (God dwelling with Israel as the Exodus's purpose). Alter notes the rhetorical movement enacts the theological argument: the specific practices exist to achieve the general purpose; the general purpose is the expression of the ultimate cosmic commitment.

The chapter's climax — 'I will be their God' — is the covenant's most concentrated self-statement. Alter notes the movement from the specific (ordination sacrifices) to the universal (covenant relationship) encodes the tabernacle's theology: every specific ritual act is grounded in and points toward the fundamental covenant reality.

Calvin on God consecrating the tent: 'God ratifies and gives effect to all the outward forms of religion, which he has appointed for the guidance of his people, by the efficacy of his Spirit.' The ceremony is necessary; the Spirit's work is what makes it effective. Human ordination (the outward form) and divine consecration (the Spirit's work) are not alternatives but cooperating causes. The visible and the invisible, the external and the internal, work together in the covenant's worship system.

Calvin: This is the most comforting promise in the whole ordinance — God himself promises to be present. All the material provisions (the tent, the furnishings, the sacrifices) are means to this end: the personal, covenantal presence of God with his people.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note on the tamid (regular daily offering) explains it became the foundational offering in Israelite worship — the morning and evening lambs that structurally anchored the entire sacrificial system. Later texts develop the tamid's theological significance: Dan 8:11–14 uses its cessation as the ultimate sign of covenant desecration; Jesus' crucifixion is theologically related to the tamid in Hebrews' argument for a better sacrifice.

The NET note traces the Exodus-as-revelation formula: 'They will know that I am the LORD their God, who brought them out of Egypt so that I might dwell among them.' The Exodus is not just liberation from slavery but the creation of the covenant relationship in which God can dwell with his people.

ppl.name: Moses↗ People
ppl.text: Moses stands at the centre of the Pentateuch’s narrative — the one man who spoke with God face to face (Exod 33:11; Num 12:8) and mediated the Sinai covenant to Israel. In Leviticus he receives the priestly legislation; in Numbers he leads, intercedes, and ultimately fails at Meribah (Num 20). The writer of Hebrews calls him "faithful in all God’s house" (Heb 3:5) while distinguishing him from Christ, the Son over the house.

ppl.name: Aaron↗ People
ppl.text: Aaron, Moses’s brother, is consecrated as Israel’s first high priest in Lev 8–9. He bears the names of the twelve tribes on his breastplate before God and enters the Most Holy Place once a year on Yom Kippur (Lev 16). His failures — the golden calf (Exod 32), challenging Moses’s authority (Num 12), complicity at Meribah (Num 20) — make him the paradigm of the imperfect priest who prefigures a better high priest (Heb 7:26–28).

ppl.name: Pharaoh
ppl.text: Pharaoh is a key figure in Scripture Walkthrough.

ppl.name: LORD
ppl.text: LORD is a key figure in Scripture Walkthrough.

ppl.name: Miriam↗ People
ppl.role: Prophetess and worship leader; Moses’s sister
ppl.text: Miriam leads Israel’s women in worship after the Red Sea crossing (Exod 15:20) and is called a prophetess. In Num 12 she challenges Moses’s unique prophetic authority alongside Aaron and is struck with ṣāraʿat — only to be healed after Moses’s seven-word intercession. She dies at Kadesh (Num 20:1), her passing noted in two terse Hebrew words before the water crisis.

ppl.name: Joshua↗ People
ppl.role: Moses’s military commander and designated successor
ppl.text: Joshua son of Nun serves as Moses’s lieutenant throughout the wilderness period — commanding at Rephidim (Exod 17), ascending Sinai with Moses (Exod 24), guarding the tent of meeting. In Num 13–14 he stands with Caleb as one of two faithful spies. He is formally commissioned in Num 27 through Moses’s hand-laying and inherits the leadership role that Moses cannot fulfil past the Jordan.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationESV"I will dwell among the people of Israel and will be their God."NIV"Then I will dwell among the Israelites and be their God. They will know that I am the Lord their God, who brought them out of Egypt so that I might dwell among them."NASB"I will dwell among the sons of Israel and will be their God."

src.title: ANE Ordination Rites
src.quote: Mesopotamian priestly initiation rites involved ritual washing, investiture, and animal sacrifice. The Aaronic ordination uses similar forms but unique content: the blood on ear, thumb, and toe is without parallel in known ANE sources.
src.note: The uniqueness underlines its distinctive covenant theology.

src.title: Daily Sacrifice
src.quote: The tāmîd was the backbone of Temple worship. Its cessation in 70 AD — when the Romans destroyed the Temple — was the defining catastrophe of ancient Judaism.
src.note: The tāmîd's importance across Israel's history reflects the theological centrality of Exod 29:38–42.

rec.text: The final fulfilment of Exod 29:45: "God's dwelling place is now among the people."

The contrast of daily sacrifices with Christ's once-for-all offering: "day after day every priest stands... but when this priest had offered for all time one sacrifice for sins, he sat down at the right hand of God."

rows.label: Ordination: The Sanctuary Consecrated by Glory
rows.text: There I will meet with the Israelites, and the tabernacle will be consecrated by my glory. The tabernacle is not made holy by the craftsmen's skill but by God's choice to meet there and consecrate it by his own presence.

rows.label: The Regular Daily Offering: The Covenant's Continuous Rhythm
rows.text: Two lambs every day, one in the morning and one at twilight. The tamid (regular offering) establishes the covenant community's daily rhythm: morning and evening, the community approaches, and God meets them there.

hebtext.note: V.29: The ordination ceremony for priests is called millûʾîm — literally "fillings." To ordain is to fill: the priest is "filled" for the role, equipped and commissioned. The image of divine filling (mālēʾ) runs through Scripture: the Spirit fills the craftsmen (35:31), Joshua (Deut 34:9), and at Pentecost the disciples (Acts 2:4) — all ordinations, all enablments for a role in God's service.

V.7,21: The anointing of Aaron with the sacred oil (shemen hammishḥāh) is the central act of priestly ordination. Māshaḥ (to smear, anoint) gives us "Messiah" (māshîaḥ) and "Christ" (christos — "anointed one"). The king (1 Sam 16:13), the High Priest, and occasionally the prophet were all anointed. Jesus received the Spirit without measure (John 3:34) — the ultimate anointing that fulfilled and surpassed all priestly, prophetic, and royal anointings.

V.14,36: The ḥaṭṭāʾt (sin/purification offering) was specifically designed to deal with inadvertent or ritual uncleanness. The word comes from the root ḥāṭāʾ (to miss the mark, to sin). The offering purifies the sanctuary as well as the offerer — a concept crucial to understanding why Christ's death was necessary not just for individual sinners but for the cosmic restoration of the defiled creation (cf. Heb 9:23; Rom 8:20-21).

V.28: The shĕlāmîm (peace/fellowship offering) was unique in that the worshipper ate part of the sacrifice. It was a shared meal between God, the priests, and the offerer. The root shālôm (peace, wholeness, completeness) names both the offering and its goal: restored relationship, shared table, communal harmony. The Lord's Supper is the new covenant shĕlāmîm — the shared meal that enacts and celebrates peace between God and his people.

V.38-42: "Offer two lambs a day as the regular burnt offering (ʿōlat tāmîd) — one in the morning, one at twilight." The daily sacrificial rhythm structured Israel's entire temporal existence around the offering of life to God. The priest who served at the morning and evening offering gave each day a sacrificial frame. Heb 7:27 draws the contrast: Christ "does not need to offer sacrifices day after day... he sacrificed for their sins once for all when he offered himself."

V.46: "They will know (yādĕʿû) that I am the LORD their God, who brought them out of Egypt so that I might dwell among them." The purpose of the Exodus and the Tabernacle is yĕdîʿāh — knowledge of God. But this is not cognitive knowledge: yādaʿ in Hebrew is covenantal, intimate, experiential knowledge (cf. Gen 4:1 where Adam "knew" Eve). The whole elaborate system of Tabernacle worship is designed to facilitate deep personal encounter with the living God.

thread.text: The anointing oil running down Aaron's beard and onto his collar — the Psalm uses this as the image of brotherly unity.
Christ's once-for-all sacrifice contrasted with the priests' daily/annual sacrifices — the ordination of Exod 29 points to the final high priest.
The execution of the ordination ceremony as commanded here — Exod 29 gives the instructions; Lev 8 records the performance.
Daily priests stand and offer repeatedly; Christ sat down after one offering. The daily sacrifice of Exod 29 is fulfilled and superseded.
"God's dwelling place is now among the people" — the final fulfilment of v.45.
"The Word became flesh and dwelt among us" — the first fulfilment of v.45.

tx.title: Ordination ritual — SP harmonization
tx.content: The SP harmonizes the ordination instructions here with the execution account in Leviticus 8, adding phrases to ensure verbal correspondence. MT preserves differences between instruction and execution. The LXX follows MT's less harmonized version.
tx.note: The SP's harmonization between Exodus 29 and Leviticus 8 is one of its most extensive cross-book operations — it treats the Pentateuch as a single document requiring internal consistency.

debate.title: Ordination: Does the Ceremony Make the Priest or Confirm Divine Appointment?

positions.name: The ceremony constitutes the priest
positions.argument: The ordination ceremony is a sacramental act that effects what it signifies: the person is not a priest before the ceremony and is a priest after it. The washing, clothing, and anointing are not merely symbols of an appointment already made; they are the means by which the appointment is executed. Holy orders is a sacrament that confers priestly character.

positions.name: The ceremony confirms and publicly marks a prior divine appointment
positions.argument: God's appointment precedes the ceremony; the ceremony publicly marks and confirms what God has already determined. Calvin reads the priestly garments and ordination as divine accommodation to human weakness — Israel needed visible, enacted signs of what God was doing spiritually. The ceremony is meaningful and necessary but not independently efficacious.

coaching.tip: Blood on the right ear, thumb, and toe (v. 20). Hearing, doing, walking—the whole person consecrated for service. Ordination touches extremities: priesthood claims the entire life.
'I will dwell among the Israelites and be their God' (v. 45). The tabernacle's purpose stated plainly: divine presence. All the gold, linen, and ritual serve one end—God living with his people.

prayer_prompt: As you reflect on the elaborate ordination ceremony — blood on the ear, thumb, and toe — consider asking God to consecrate your hearing, your work, and your walk so that every part of your life is set apart for His purposes.
